summary: Added a new Rust workspace tool, `melee-flags`, that parses C-style integer literals and emits copyable C declarations expressing the set bits as `1 << n` flag expressions. The tool supports hex, binary, octal, and decimal inputs, an optional `--signed` mode, automatic 32-bit versus 64-bit type selection, and comment-form warnings for invalid inputs.
what_changed: Added `tools/flags` as a Cargo workspace member in the root `Cargo.toml`.
Created the `melee-flags` Rust crate with package metadata, AGPL-3.0-or-later licensing, and dependencies on `anyhow`, `clap` with derive support, `lazy_static`, and `regex`.
Implemented a CLI that accepts positional `FLAGS` arguments plus `-s`/`--signed`.
Implemented parsing for C-like integer literal forms: `0x` hexadecimal, `0b` binary, leading-zero octal, and decimal.
Implemented output formatting that decomposes each parsed integer into bitshift terms joined by `|`, such as `(1 << 0) | (1 << 4)`.
Added C literal suffix selection for shift bases: `1`, `1U`, `1L`, or `1UL` depending on the mask range.
Selected output declaration type based on value range and signed mode: `i32`, `u32`, `i64`, or `u64`.
For invalid input, emitted a Doxygen-style warning comment rather than stopping the entire batch: `/// @warning <input>: Invalid input.`
Added a standalone AGPL license file under `tools/flags/LICENSE`.

Updated `Cargo.lock`, adding the new crate and dependency graph including `clap`, `clap_derive`, `clap_lex`, `lazy_static`, `regex`, and related transitive packages; the lockfile also bumped `serde` from 1.0.155 to 1.0.157.
smart_moves: The tool directly targets a common decomp workflow: converting observed integer masks into explicit flag-bit expressions that can be pasted into C source while preserving readability.
Using `clap::Parser` gives the tool standard `--help`, `--version`, and argument parsing behavior with very little custom code.
The regex uses explicit capture groups ordered by literal precedence, making radix detection simple and avoiding ambiguous parsing between hex, binary, octal, and decimal forms.
Invalid inputs become warning comments in the generated output, which lets users process mixed batches without losing valid conversions.
The formatter handles zero specially as `0`, avoiding an empty bit-expression list.
The shift base suffix logic avoids generating expressions like `1 << 31` or `1 << 32` with the wrong C integer width/sign behavior by switching to `1U`, `1L`, or `1UL` at wider masks.
The `--signed` flag changes declaration type selection while leaving the bit decomposition behavior consistent.
decomp_lessons: Small repo-local CLI tools can remove repetitive manual work from decompilation, especially when translating raw constants into named or inspectable bit flags.
When producing C snippets from tooling, preserve the original input in a leading comment so later reviewers can audit how each generated declaration was derived.
For bitmask reconstruction, generate individual `1 << bit` terms rather than a single hex literal when the goal is to identify, name, or compare flags during reverse engineering.
C integer promotion and literal width matter for high bits; generated shifts should use suffixes appropriate to the bit position.
Batch tools should degrade gracefully on malformed inputs so one bad copied token does not prevent useful output for the rest of the list.
Adding tooling as a Cargo workspace member keeps invocation consistent with other Rust tools, e.g. `cargo run -rp melee-flags -- ...`.
naming_conventions: Rust workspace tool crates use a `melee-` prefix; this PR adds `melee-flags`.
The generated C declarations are named sequentially as `flags0`, `flags1`, `flags2`, etc.
Generated declarations follow the pattern `static <type> const flagsN = <bit expression>;`.
The CLI option for signed output is named `signed` and exposed as both `-s` and `--signed`.
Internal parser constants are named `FLAGS_RE` and `FLAGS_RADIX`.
assembly_or_matching_tactics: Use the tool to turn raw mask constants found during decompilation into explicit bit positions, which can help identify flag fields and compare against assembly bit tests.
For constants with high bits set, preserve C correctness by using generated suffixes such as `1U << 31`, `1L << 32`, and `1UL` for larger unsigned ranges.
Use signed mode when the target decompiled declaration needs signed typedef-style output (`i32`/`i64`), and default mode for unsigned flag masks (`u32`/`u64`).
review_feedback: No issue comments, review comments, or review records were present in the provided PR slice, so no reviewer feedback can be extracted.
follow_up_queries: Find later PRs that modify `tools/flags` or `melee-flags`.
Search for generated `flags0` or `static u32 const flags` snippets in later decomp work.
Check whether later tooling standardized Rust crate licensing or removed per-tool AGPL license files.
Look for future review discussions about C literal suffixes for high-bit shifts.
